deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—why the Socratic approach, why asking the student to derive rankings or find sources themselves, supports learning. The tutoring rationale is never articulated.

In Transcript 1, after the student successfully arrives at the strong four-field framework in Turn 10 following several rounds of revision, the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the revised approach worked better. Similarly in Transcripts 2 and 3, when students reach correct conclusions after earlier confusion, the tutor advances without prompting process reflection.

grade.overview: The tutor performs strongly across all three transcripts, consistently using Socratic questioning, building on student responses, maintaining appropriate tone, and keeping responses concise and interactive. The primary weaknesses are the absence of any explicit tutoring rationale explanation and missed opportunities to prompt process reflection after student improvement.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never gives submission-ready answers, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets students generate key ideas through guided questioning. 1.2 is fully intact: the tutor builds on each correct student step and diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never explained across any of the three conversations (1.3.A.a), and after students successfully revise their thinking—most clearly in Transcript 1 Turn 10 and Transcript 2 Turn 8—the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong, with no meaningful repetition, no off-topic drift, no monologuing, and a consistently supportive, non-evaluative tone.

transcripts.context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the city data table for my case study city, and I’m kinda stuck on what counts as the key “existing conditions.” Can u help me figure out what info I should prioritize first?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—start with the items the assignment explicitly requires, then ask which ones most shape climate risk in your city. 

First priority: location, population, physical area, and Koppen climate zone. Why might those four give you the fastest picture of existing conditions?

After that, add population race/ethnicity, income per capita, and GDP to understand who may be most affected and what capacity the city has to respond.

What city did you choose? If you tell me, I can help you decide which 2–3 data points matter most to gather first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief and use guided discovery rather than listing a full solution. To move the student toward the assignment requirements, I will anchor the discussion in the specific fields named in the prompt and ask a focused question that helps them prioritize information based on climate vulnerability and action planning. I will not write the table for the student or provide a complete answer; instead I will help them decide an order of research.

exchanges.student: I picked Phoenix, but idk why physical area matters that much tbh. Is it mostly for density stuff, or am I missing something?
exchanges.tutor:
Density is one reason, yes—but don’t stop there.

For a city like Phoenix, what climate problems get harder when a city covers a very large area: heat, water delivery, transportation, emergency response, land cover, or all of those?

And if you combine physical area with population, what 1 metric can you derive that tells you something important about urban form?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's question, then use Socratic prompts to help them infer why physical area matters for Phoenix specifically. I will avoid giving a complete explanation and instead connect area to density, exposure, infrastructure spread, and service provision, all relevant to climate vulnerability in this assignment. The response will stay brief and on-task.

exchanges.student: I think it’s all of those lol, and population + area gives density, right? I’m just not sure how to connect density back to climate vulnerability for Phoenix rn.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, density. Good.

Now push one step further: in Phoenix, is climate risk mainly about too many people packed together, or about a hot metro area spreading across lots of paved land with heavy car dependence and uneven shade?

How might low density change 3 things: surface heat, infrastructure demand, and access to cooling? Try answering those in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student's correct inference without completing the analysis for them. Then I will ask a sequence of narrow questions that pushes them to connect density and urban form to Phoenix-specific climate risks, especially heat and water, while keeping the response concise and assignment-focused.

exchanges.student: I think Phoenix is more the second one—spread out, lots of pavement, cars, and not enough shade. Low density prob makes heat worse, needs more infrastructure over bigger space, and maybe cooling access gets uneven depending on neighborhood?
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right direction.

Now sort your points into the table’s bigger purpose: which of those are physical conditions, which are demographic equity conditions, and which hint at investment priorities?

Try naming 1 data item you already have for each bucket for Phoenix.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's progress, then use one forward-moving Socratic prompt to help them turn ideas into assignment-ready categories without writing for them. This keeps the help minimal and focused on the city data table and the larger purpose of identifying vulnerabilities and priorities.

exchanges.student: Uh maybe physical = Koppen climate zone, demographic = race/ethnicity, and investment priority = income per capita? I’m kinda guessing on the last one.
exchanges.tutor:
Partly right, but you’re mixing data with interpretation.

The table should hold data points; “investment priority” is usually something you infer from them.

Ask yourself: which table fields help you infer where vulnerability may be concentrated in Phoenix? Think about climate exposure, population distribution, and economic capacity. Which 2–3 fields best support that inference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's partial success, correct the weak categorization without supplying a finished answer, and guide them to distinguish between raw data fields and analytic conclusions. This follows Socratic method and keeps the response short while moving them toward the assignment's purpose.

exchanges.student: Maybe climate zone, population density, and income per capita? Ngl I’m not fully sure if GDP matters here or if it’s too broad.
exchanges.tutor:
Those 3 are more useful than GDP for vulnerability at first pass.

Now ask: income per capita tells you about whom, and GDP tells you about what? Are those the same scale?

For Phoenix, which is more helpful for locating vulnerable residents, and which is more about the city’s overall capacity to act?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the reply concise, validate the student's progress, and guide them to distinguish city-scale capacity from household vulnerability without directly deciding for them. This advances their reasoning about which required fields matter and how to use them for Phoenix.

exchanges.student: I think income per capita is more about residents and who might struggle more, and GDP is more like overall city economic capacity. So for vulnerable people, income per capita seems more useful, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s a solid distinction.

So don’t treat GDP as useless; treat it as secondary. The assignment requires it, but it may be less central than income for your first interpretation.

If you had to rank the required fields for Phoenix from most immediately useful to least for understanding climate vulnerability, what would your top 4 be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's distinction, then nudge them to avoid dropping a required field entirely by asking them to decide primary versus secondary priority. This preserves guided discovery and keeps the answer bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I’d say climate zone, population, physical area, and income per capita. Does that sound reasonable, or should race/ethnicity be in the top 4 instead?
exchanges.tutor:
Reasonable, but test it instead of guessing.

Which field helps you see uneven vulnerability within Phoenix more clearly: total population or race/ethnicity?

And which one is more basic context that you almost always need no matter the city? Decide those roles, then rank again.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid directly deciding the ranking for the student. Instead, I’ll push them to compare two fields using the assignment’s purpose: understanding vulnerability and prioritization. This keeps the response brief, Socratic, and tied to Phoenix and the required table fields.

exchanges.student: Yeah ok, total population is basic context, but race/ethnicity prob shows uneven vulnerability better. So maybe top 4 is climate zone, income per capita, race/ethnicity, then population?
exchanges.tutor:
Better, but you may have dropped one too quickly: physical area.

For Phoenix, if sprawl and heat exposure matter, can you really interpret population without area?

Revise your top 4 one more time, and this time make sure each field has a distinct job: climate exposure, social vulnerability, scale/urban form, and baseline context.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's improved reasoning while pushing them to notice a missing required field that is analytically important for Phoenix. I will not give a final ranked answer; instead I will ask a targeted question that helps them revise their own top 4 and connect it to vulnerability. This stays brief, uses guided discovery, and keeps the focus on the assignment.

exchanges.student: Ok yeah, then maybe climate zone = exposure, income per capita = social vulnerability, physical area = urban form, and population = baseline context. That makes more sense now.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s a strong framework.

Now use it: for each of those 4 fields, write a 5–8 word note on why it matters for Phoenix. Don’t make it polished—just functional.

After that, decide where race/ethnicity fits: replace one of the 4, or add it as a fifth equity field?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student’s progress and prompt them to apply the framework immediately to the assignment, without writing the content for them. This keeps the response concise, Socratic, and focused on moving from understanding to action.

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m working on the city data table for my case study city, and I’m kinda stuck on what counts as the main categories rn. can u help me figure out how to organize the location, population, income, GDP, and Köppen climate stuff?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—start by grouping the required data into 3 buckets: 
1. Geographic: location, physical area, Köppen climate zone
2. Demographic: total population, race/ethnicity breakdown
3. Economic: income per capita, GDP

Then ask yourself: for each category, what exact unit or year will you report so the table stays consistent?

What city did you choose, and which of those 3 buckets feels least clear right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in the tutor role and use guided discovery rather than filling in the table for the student. To keep the response bite-sized and useful, I’ll help the student organize the required categories into a simple structure, then ask a forward-moving Socratic question that prompts them to apply it to their chosen city. I will not provide a completed table or write their assignment for them.

exchanges.student: I picked Mumbai, and tbh the economic bucket is the messiest for me. idk what year to use or whether GDP should be city-only vs metro.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—those are exactly the right questions.

Before choosing a number, ask: what geographic unit is your case study actually about—municipal Mumbai, Greater Mumbai, or the metro region? Your GDP should match that unit as closely as possible.

For year, what’s the most recent year you can find that also lets your income and population data stay reasonably consistent?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student focused on the assignment and use Socratic questioning to help them resolve the ambiguity around economic indicators. I will not choose the year or GDP definition for them; instead I’ll guide them to prioritize consistency, source transparency, and geographic matching across indicators. This supports the assignment without completing it for them.

exchanges.student: I think it should be Greater Mumbai, but I’m not 100% sure if that’s the same as the municipal area. for the year, maybe I should just use whatever is newest?
exchanges.tutor:
Not automatically. “Newest” can create a messy table if every variable comes from a different boundary or year.

First question: when your course says “city,” which boundary will your other data likely use most often?

Second: if GDP is only available for metro but population/income are for municipal Mumbai, would that make your table stronger or weaker?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will guide the student to distinguish between administrative units and avoid the oversimplified 'newest is always best' approach. I’ll use short Socratic prompts that move toward a defensible table setup: define the city boundary first, then choose a year strategy based on consistency and comparability. I will not supply the solution or write wording for them.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, weaker prob, bc then I’d be comparing diff things. I’m guessing I should pick the boundary that most sources use for population and area, right?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the stronger approach.

So make your rule explicit: choose one city boundary first, then prefer data that matches it.

Now test your rule: if Mumbai population, area, and income all align to one boundary, but GDP doesn’t, what do you think is better—use a mismatched GDP anyway, or note a limitation and look for a closer proxy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student’s correct reasoning and push them one step further toward a defensible method without supplying the completed answer. The response stays brief, Socratic, and centered on the assignment by having them articulate a boundary-first rule and consider what to do when one variable does not align.

exchanges.student: I think I should note the limitation and find a closer proxy if I can. ngl I’m still fuzzy on what would count as a “closer proxy” for GDP tho.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. A closer proxy is one that better matches your chosen boundary, even if it’s not perfect.

Ask yourself: what makes a GDP figure “close” for your table—same boundary, similar year, and a clear source, right?

Which is closer to your case: a state-level number, a metro-region number, or an estimate tied to Greater Mumbai?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the student on the assignment and use guided discovery to help them reason about proxies without naming a specific source or solving the table. The question sequence narrows the concept: a good proxy should match the chosen boundary, be transparent, and be comparable in year/units. This remains bite-sized and does not write the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: an estimate tied to Greater Mumbai sounds closest, then metro, then state-level last. is it ok if I put a note in the table saying GDP is an estimate and boundary definitions vary?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that would strengthen the table, not weaken it.

Transparency matters more than pretending the data are perfectly aligned.

What 3 pieces of context should your note include so a reader can judge the GDP figure properly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's reasoning and guide them toward transparent documentation practices without drafting the note for them. This follows the assignment focus and avoids giving wording. I’ll ask a question that helps them decide what minimal metadata belongs in the table.

exchanges.student: maybe: the year, the geographic boundary, and the source? or also that it’s an estimate, idk if that’s cheating lol
exchanges.tutor:
That’s not cheating. Hiding uncertainty would be worse.

Your instinct is solid. Now refine it: which matters more for interpretation—just the source, or source + the fact that the number is an estimate based on a specific boundary?

List the minimum note elements you think are essential.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reassure the student that disclosure is appropriate, not cheating, and prompt them to identify the essential note elements themselves. I will avoid writing the note and keep the response concise and corrective, in line with the tutor role.

exchanges.student: ok, minimum would be: year, boundary used, source, and that it’s an estimate. I think that makes the GDP row make more sense.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. That’s a defensible setup.

Now zoom out: can you apply that same consistency rule to every row—variable, year, boundary, source, and any note if needed?

Which remaining row for Mumbai feels most likely to cause the same kind of boundary problem?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will acknowledge the student's progress and shift them from conceptual uncertainty toward action on the assignment. To preserve guided discovery, I’ll ask them to apply the same consistency logic to the remaining categories rather than giving a completed framework. This keeps the response bite-sized and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: probably population race/ethnicity breakdown, bc idk if Mumbai data is even reported that way. can u help me think thru what to do if that exact category isn’t available?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—but don’t force a bad fit. Ask: does Mumbai commonly report identity in the same race/ethnicity terms, or through different social categories?

If the exact category isn’t available, what would be more honest: leave a note that comparable race/ethnicity data are unavailable, or use a locally relevant demographic proxy and label it clearly?

Which local categories have you seen in your sources?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I’ll guide the student to handle a missing required category by thinking about comparability, local relevance, and transparent substitution, without supplying a completed category or wording. I’ll keep it short and ask questions that move toward a defensible table decision.

exchanges.student: I haven’t looked deep yet, but I’m guessing religion, language, or caste might show up more. I think a labeled local proxy makes more sense than pretending it’s race/ethnicity.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right instinct. Don’t import a category that doesn’t fit the local context.

Now be stricter: which proxy is most consistently reported for Mumbai, and which one would actually help explain climate vulnerability or inequality in the city?

Pick one to investigate first rather than chasing all three. Which one seems strongest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student’s reasoning and push them to choose a defensible proxy by evaluating relevance to the assignment’s goal. I will avoid naming the final category or drafting table language, and instead ask targeted questions that help them decide among religion, language, or caste based on availability, comparability, and connection to urban vulnerability.

transcripts.context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
transcripts.exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on the city data table rn 😅 i picked a case study city, but idk how to find stuff like GDP and the Köppen climate zone.
exchanges.tutor:
Start with source types, not answers. For GDP, ask yourself: is your city likely to publish metro/regional economic statistics, or will you need a national statistics office, World Bank-style dataset, or OECD-style metro data?

For Köppen, what kind of source would classify climate zones on a map rather than in a city report? Think atlas, climate classification map, or research dataset.

What city did you choose? Tell me that, and I’ll help you narrow down the best places to search without filling the table for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in tutor role, keep the response brief, and use guided discovery rather than supplying the data directly. Since the student is stuck on where to find specific categories, I should help them identify credible source types and a search process, then ask a forward-moving question so they do the actual finding. This supports the assignment goal of building the Geographic and Demographic Data Table without completing it for them.

exchanges.student: I chose Jakarta. GDP probs isn’t from the city website then? For Köppen, I’m guessing I need some climate map source, but idk which ones are legit.
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct: Jakarta GDP may not sit neatly on a city website. First question: does your assignment mean the city proper, or would a metro/provincial figure be the closest available proxy?

For legit sources, ask: who would officially measure economic output in Indonesia—city government, national statistics agency, or an international database?

For Köppen, what would make a source credible: a peer-reviewed/research-based classification map, or a random infographic? Try naming one source type you think is strongest for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's direction, then narrow the search process through Socratic questions that move toward credible source selection. I will avoid giving the exact sources or data outright, and instead prompt the student to distinguish between city-level and metro/provincial GDP and between popular maps and authoritative climate datasets. This keeps the help bite-sized and assignment-focused.

exchanges.student: I think metro/provincial might be the proxy, yeah. Strongest source types seem like national stats agency for GDP and a research-based climate classification map for Köppen, not some random site.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—that’s the right direction.

Now make it operational: what search terms would you use for the Indonesian statistics agency and for a Jakarta economic output figure? Be specific.

And for Köppen, what detail do you need to verify besides the zone label itself—city location on the map, legend, and publication source? Write one search query for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's reasoning and push them one step further with concrete but still discovery-based prompts. I should not provide the sources directly; instead I will help them identify what search terms and source characteristics to look for, which advances the assignment without doing it for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe: “Indonesia statistics agency Jakarta GRDP” or “BPS Jakarta regional GDP.” For Köppen maybe “Jakarta Köppen climate classification map peer reviewed” bc I need the map, legend, and source too.
exchanges.tutor:
Those are solid queries. Now slow down and check comparability.

For GDP/GRDP, what exact unit and year will your table use, and will you note if it’s city, province, or metro proxy?

For Köppen, once you find a map, how will you justify that the zone applies to Jakarta specifically rather than Indonesia generally?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will confirm the student's search strategy and push them to refine definitions and units, because the main risk now is collecting mismatched figures. I will keep it brief and Socratic, asking questions that move directly toward a usable table entry without giving specific data or sources.

exchanges.student: uhhh I guess I should pick one year and keep it consistent, plus note if it’s province not city. For Köppen, I’d need to locate Jakarta on the map and match it to the legend, not just say “Indonesia is tropical.”
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly. That’s the difference between a careful table and a sloppy one.

Next step: find one GDP/GRDP source and one Köppen source, then report back only these four things for each: source name, year, geographic unit, and why it fits your table.

Do that before chasing more categories.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified the correct methodological checks. I should reinforce that and ask for a concrete next action so they move from abstract understanding to actually filling the table. I will not provide the answer, only structure their next step.

exchanges.student: ok yeah, that helps. I’ll find one BPS-type GRDP source and one research climate map, then I’ll check back w/ source name, year, geo unit, and why it fits.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s a workable plan.

When you come back, include the exact table wording you plan to use for each entry and note any limitation, like “province-level proxy.” That will keep your data defensible.

Go find those two sources first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should acknowledge the student's plan, keep the response brief, and maintain momentum without adding unnecessary detail. A concise checklist reinforces the method and keeps the student focused on the assignment rather than broad detours. I will not provide sources or answers, only a clear standard for what to bring back.

exchanges.student: got it — I’ll bring back the wording too, plus any limitation notes. thx, this finally feels less messy.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. You’ve reduced the problem to a manageable checklist.

Now go verify the two sources. When you return, we’ll test whether your wording is precise enough for the table and whether your limitation notes are honest and useful.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will briefly reinforce the student’s progress and direct them to the next concrete action, staying within the assignment and not expanding into unrelated help. This maintains guided discovery and keeps the interaction bite-sized.

exchanges.student: ok, I’ll do that rn. if I get stuck on whether a source is “close enough,” I’ll ask.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly. And when you ask, don’t just send the link—tell me what makes you think it is or isn’t close enough.

Use this test: official or research-based, clear year, clear geography, and usable for your table. If one of those is weak, say so.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should encourage the student to return with evidence and frame a simple criterion for source suitability without preemptively solving it. This stays Socratic, concise, and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah that rubric helps. I’ll check those 4 things and flag whatever feels shaky.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s the right standard.

Go test your sources against those four checks, and come back with the shaky parts only. We’ll sort those out without losing the bigger picture of the table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will close this turn by reinforcing the student's evaluation criteria and keeping them focused on the immediate assignment task. I should not add more scaffolding than necessary or provide any direct solution. A brief prompt to return with evidence supports guided discovery and maintains momentum.

exchanges.student: kk, I’ll come back w/ just the shaky bits. hopefully less guessing this time.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the goal: less guessing, more defensible choices.

Bring the uncertain parts, and be ready to say which of the four checks each source passes or fails. Then we can tighten the table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should end with brief encouragement and a clear expectation for the next turn. This keeps the student focused on evaluating sources for the Jakarta data table and avoids unnecessary detours or direct answers.
